transcript.text: Hey, how's it going?
Yeah, pretty well.
Feels like a Friday with Thanksgiving tomorrow.
You traveling at all for Thanksgiving?
Thank heavens no.
How about you?
Yeah, I will be traveling to my parents and then also my fiance's parents, but they're all in the Houston area, so that makes it easy.
Just got to be prepared to eat a lot of food.
Okay, that's a tall task, but I think you're up for it.
Oh, you bet.
This meeting is being recorded.
Yeah, I'm in San Francisco, and I mean, I guess I'll go half an hour, know, so I'm driving a bit easy, so no complaints.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
Yeah, I lived in SF for three years, right out of college.
But, you know, when you start to get further along in life, you just want to optimize for having the built-in babysitters, right?
Right.
That's funny.
That is funny.
Cool.
That's great.
Cool.
Awesome.
Well, excited to connect now.
Maybe I'll start with a little bit of context and I'd to just hear more.
I mean, the quick context is, yeah, I'm part of Francisco Partners and what's called a marketing operating partner.
So really, I helped.

Just the portfolio companies just kind of manage and make sense of all the marketing opportunities that are out there, and AI search, surprise, surprise, is a biggie, right, come on fast and furious, really important for almost every company, and it's so new and evolving, so that's been a big kind of area of focus just for me and just almost every single portfolio company, you know, and sort of, I don't know, close to six months now, or maybe more now, I sort of got to know X Funnel, you know, in Beheri over there, and maybe one or two other people.

And originally, he was kind of on a short list of companies that I would, or partners I would suggest to the portfolio companies, hey, you know, here's a couple you should look at, and again and again, X Funnel, like, won those little bake-offs, you know, and everyone's like, oh, we want to work with X Funnel, I'm like, okay, cool, so that kind of told me, all right, it's a good partner, you know, and he and the team, they have been a good partner to a bunch of Francisco Partners companies, we had an in-person conference, we had an in an-person conference, it was all going so well, and then
And then, sorry, the fucker gets bought by HubSpot, God damn it.
Like, usually I'm like, I'm worried that some startups are going to go out of business and I'm like, I'm going to manage that risk.
The good startup getting bought was not a risk I anticipated, but in hindsight, it totally makes sense.
And he's been cool about it and he's not leaving his hire driver or anything, but it's the real need that we have.
And the reality is there's many companies in the portfolio, all at different stages of maturity and sophistication.
So it's a real range of needs that are out there.
So I'm kind of in the process of identifying, you know, a new set of resources for portfolio companies to be able to kind of go to, depending on what their needs are.
Anyway, that's the setup.
That's the context.
Yeah.
Yeah, super helpful.
And just intro on my side, I'm the founding AE.
I work hand in hand with Marcel, our CEO.
And a lot of times, like my job is just to create some.
It's based on his calendar, so he can focus on our initiatives with the products, because right now we're like an end-to-end partner, like an agency almost.
We'll do everything from SEO and GEO strategy, we create content for all of our clients, we stage and publish in their CMS and do all the LLM visibility and monitoring.
So like traditional SEO agency, they're just creating some briefs or maybe executing on some content and doing strategy.
We're like really end-to-end and our clients see us as an extension of their content team.
And like the alternative is, you know, I mean everyone wants to show up in AI search, but also everybody's thinking about how can we leverage AI to create content faster?
But how can it like not be, have the appearance of being AI generated?
Yeah.
And so like, what we do is we have these really sophisticated AI workflows, I'll show you under the hood.
We've got experts in the loop, like our chief content officer, he was the editor-in-chief at TechCrunch for over a decade, all the people that we hire on our delivery team.

Like they're SEO and content marketing experts, and so we're helping teams who, you know, maybe they're like, I want to leverage AI, but how long is it going to take me to build all these workflows, like, how do I make sure I update them when models change, are the costs going to scale out of control as we're processing all these tokens, it's solving for that time to value, and it's being able to scale quality in a way that's like, on brand for the customer, and then making sure the content's created in a way that's optimized for LLM.
So like, throwing a ton at you right now, we'll make more sense of it.
Initial reactions, questions.

Yeah, so that made that helpful, that's an important piece of the puzzle, it's, you know, kind of high quality authoritative content at scale, like definitely, you know, so like that is good to hear, but just get a more basic level, what are your, I mean, you sort of said almost an agency or agency-like, can you kind of just describe a little bit more about what the engagements look like, you know, like, you know, who's involved, people involved, is their technology involved, what's the, what are.
What the interactions look like?
That kind of stuff.
Yeah, you bet.
So we'll do an eight week strategy sprint as part of the phase one in our engagements.
And then clients have the ability to continue like they can opt out into phase two, which is a 12 month, what we call growth execution.
So we ingest resources ahead of the kickoff call.
And then the meetings the client has to join is an hour kickoff and a 45 minute product deep dive.
And then we'll maybe have like a quick 15 to 30 minute meeting with the person in charge of the CMS so that we can get to a place where we're self sufficient with staging and publishing.
And so deliverables in the strategy sprint, it's these artifacts that give our workflows, the context engineering that's necessary to be really relevant and frankly effective.
And it's also being able to create 10 to 20 published pieces so that you can see the quality we hit.
And then like a three to six month content roadmap.
So you see like what we would focus on in phase two, in phase two.
We target five articles per week.
We, for every client, start off on editorial.
So that's their blog.
That could be like a new section of the site, like guides.
And then from there, we start thinking about how can we move into programmatic?
How could we move into a work stream that's connected to editorial, like a newsletter, where maybe you're trying to build more distribution.
And so, you know, for some concrete examples here, Lovable, we're building out their templates library.
And we're using like their API and their product to programmatically create these different apps or templates, similar to like what a content management system might want to create.
And we actually did like the web development so that the design, so that like Lovable now has templates that their customers or their interested prospects can remix, can preview.
And everything that we've created, you know, the text is all optimized.

So we've done this example for Lovable, and then we've also done the entire vendor directory for Ramp, and so each one of these pages for Ramp, like they want to be the authority for business spending.
You have some poster child clients, nicely done, yeah.

Yeah, I mean, I think it really just comes down to our founder and CEO, Marcel, he's pretty well known in this space, and he's got a story of every stop in his career as a marketing leader, creating an organic content engine.
And he's just not one to spend on paid.
Like he's always believed brands should be publishers, whether it was, you know, HashiCorp and Service Titan as they were going through hyper growth and scale as they got acquired by Meta.
I think like he just built the product that he always wanted as a CMO.
And so that's helped us in terms of like, who we're working with, and yeah, here's a lot of the other logos of clients that we're working with right now.
Yeah.
Okay.
And say a little bit more.
I mean, don't um
I mean, again, Ramp and Lovebowl are just whatever, total high-flyers.
I mean, listening to you, it really sounds like you're doing most of it.
Like, obviously, there's an alignment kind of period where you align, but then you have a lot of independence and just you're off on your own.
Is that, did I get that right?
Yeah.
Yeah, good question.
What we want our clients to have to do is basically just review the drafts that we create, right?
Give timely feedback, help point us in the right direction to become better experts on their products and services.

But we're actually, to your point, creating, like, doing all the keyword analysis, connecting that to topics where it makes sense to have authority, generating assignments across all those different topics, and keeping, like, a huge backlog of articles that they can consider.
And then the partnership really just becomes us sending.
You know, Google Docs over on a weekly basis, getting feedback and getting approvals from their team and then going out and handling like the publishing.
So, yeah, there's, you know, a little, a very small time commitment when it comes to the partnership.
And the key is just in phase one of the engagement, being able to closely collaborate so that we can become experts faster.

And like, as we generate these artifacts, so, you know, take engine in the business travel space, we've created these artifacts around their writing guidelines, which, again, we can do through just looking at the brand guidelines and write tone and voice docs, but it helps to capture some of that nuance, you know, for like engine, they target different industry verticals.
And so we created these personas artifacts across each industry.
And then like, we're getting inside the mind of who they go after, who their ICP is, like, what are their fears and uncertainties?
What are their motivations?
And so this is all connected to like the topic clusters that we create.
But we would want the team to weigh in on, okay, there's 10 topic clusters, three out of those 10, like, we don't want to spend our time here, right?
Like, we want to spend our time on these three to five.
So make sure you focus on generating assignments on those three to five, not the other five.
That's how some of the calibration and some of the work from the client looks like.
Yeah, a lot of questions.
Try to keep them focused.

It's just, I'm anticipating, I'm just imagining just existing companies I work with, all of them, you know, have, you know, fairly robust, you know, kind of SEO programs, because most of the companies are kind of B2B SaaS, right?
So that's the category.
So they have SEO, they've understood that, they're not as high flying as level of ramp, but they, but this has been a focus, or they're, but they've been around for a lot longer too.
And they have SEO teams.
Just like, that's the question that comes to mind, you know, just can you talk to me about that?
Like how, how you guys play nicely?
With existing SEO teams, because it feels like there might be some overlap, and I'm just curious if that's come up at all, you know?
Yeah, that's a good question.
I would say almost every client we're working with has a need for scale to some extent.
So like maybe they're creating two blogs a week, and then we can get them closer to five to ten.
Or maybe they're not doing any refreshes, or they're refreshing like once a year instead of every three months.
It's really figuring out what's the lane where we can execute and not intrude on anyone's territory.
And a lot of times that's, well, we're publishing blogs today, but we can't publish more because we'd have to hire more folks.
Or like we can't get to the quality using AI.
And so that's how I think we work well with like SEO teams, with content teams, is there's like a need to create more content.
And there's also a gap in their ability to do that with their existing resources.
Okay, so it's just more.
We all want more.
Hey, existing SEO team, you're going to do whatever you're going to do.
We're going to stay in sync.

And then we're just going to do more, yeah, yeah, exactly, I mean, for Ramp, right, like, the vendor directory was a gap, they had like no way to go out and execute at scale on all these different software companies that they want to give buyers good content and context around, and they want to be the authority for everything business spending, and so now, like, Ramp shows up when you search ClickUp versus Airtable.
And that's like the last thing you would think Ramp would show up for, right, you'd think it would be like, how to process an ACH transaction.
So it's like, what's that lane where we can execute, you know, and can we start an editorial, because if we start an editorial, we can be more successful in the engagement.
And then we can move to different pieces like this per diem calculator that we created for Ramp, there's a page for every city in America.
So if someone's thinking about what's the average per diem in Houston or San Francisco, Ramp has...
It's a page for each city that we created, it's thinking about, we created like this expense classifier for ramps.
So if someone searched what expense category is office furniture or like any type of expense, we've got a page that's more optimized for SEO and GEO.

And so maybe like for a lot of the clients, it's like there's a huge need on the refresh side, adding FAQs, doing better crosslinking, thinking about content gaps that they're not executing on because they just don't have the content creators in house, replacing an SEO agency who's only doing a piece of this and maybe not end to end.
There's no means to say like, we can't do what SEO teams are doing from a strategy perspective, but can we help them with their output?
Can we help them figure out gaps that they're not thinking about today that maybe aren't as obvious?
That would be other spots, I think, where we can fit in.
Yeah.

And so it's fine, but just to observe, there's definitely some coordination, you know, and so, you know, really getting to know those existing SEO teams and understanding where they're working and defining, okay, great, you know, you're over there in that area, then we're going to go over in this different area, and that's fine, just sort of, like, articulating what's important, guess, you know.
Yeah, definitely.
I mean, if, you know, someone hired us and they said, like, hey, just go for it, right, we don't know what we want to create, that's totally fine.

But for, like, larger companies, it's, like, much more collaborative, you know, like Webflow, it was, hey, our developer relations team, they need a ton of help with content, and so we do a lot of their docs, we do their integrations pages.
And that was, like, a specific place that made sense to start the partnership, and now they're interested in, like, hey, help us with this made in Webflow templates library.
We love what you did for RAMP, we love what you're doing for Lovable, we want to step into that space also.
So, you know, editorial is, like, the most common

The starting point, but larger, more established companies, a lot of times it's like, well, where's the gap that you just don't feel confident about using AI to create content, or the scale that you can hit with like internal resources.
Okay.
Okay.
Can you talk a little bit about like, you know, you know, you guys are doing high quality work, and sort of it's important.
I'm sure there's a price tag there.
But but how are how are your, your, your clients?
Saying to themselves and telling their CFOs, like, yes, this has been totally worth it.
This has been a great investment.
Yeah, for sure.
I would say that, you know, like, they're pointing to their traffic, they're pointing to their content engagement, they're pointing to their conversions.
And so we're working on some case studies that we're publishing, like, early in Q1, but a lot of times, you know, we'll show different SEMrush graphs, and kind of point out, like, with augment code.

We started working in June, they've since tripled their organic traffic, and then we set up a Looker dashboard for each client where we can connect it to conversions, engagement, and then also, you know, we've got some examples where we can go searches and, like, point to the fact that, you know, our articles are showing up several times in a lot of those searches, not just one citation, but, like, multiple citations of articles that we've We've created, and then we've also got, like, some proof points around, you know, the specific quantity of citations that we helped drive for a company or the specific number of leads, like, we've helped them generate, but, you know, the initial connection to GEO, I think, is, like, on the top of every CMO's radar, and so from there, it's just, like, building confidence in the way that our workflows are involved in creating content, and then showing those examples where we've driven, we've driven,
And so intent traffic much more quickly than, you know, anything they've seen.

Maybe then just going back a couple of topics, just an issue that's definitely come up, I'm just curious how you think about it, is the need to be, to really have a lot of expertise, a lot of deep expertise.
I'm just giving an anecdote, this is about four years ago, actually I worked for like a high-end bicycle company, and I ride a bike, but I don't ride high-end bikes.
And so we wanted to scale our SEO, and so we hired a bunch of kind of freelance writers to, to write about high-end bikes and what's important and stuff.
And I read this stuff, like, oh, that's fine, it's a meme.
But like the bike snobs, the bike nerves were like, this is .
This, this person doesn't know diddly.
They're like, this is ridiculous.
I'm like, I don't know, it seems fine to me.
And obviously it's different for every case, but so many of the companies I work with are pretty technical, like B2B SaaS businesses.
And so just talk to me, how, how do you kind of develop that expertise that
that's needed to be truly authoritative, yeah.
Yeah, definitely a really good question.

I would say that's one sweet spot that we found, like with augment code, everything you see in their guides section we've published, and a lot of this content is pretty technical and even is like using code snippets throughout the content.
If you think about like some of the customers that we're working with, know, Sentinel-1, Abnormal Security, Airbyte, with Airbyte, we've done all of these data engineering resources.
So, like, what's the best way to handle data deduplication and ETL?
And so, like, what we do is, we create those artifacts, and I can go to Augment Codes workspace, which I think will give you some better examples.
So we create these initial...
And we can point the research that we do in specific areas.
So with the proofreader checklist, we're actually specifying where we want to go out and do the research.
With the personas artifacts, we're actually getting deep into what matters to the folks that they're creating content for.
Who are they?
How long have they been in their roles?
Or how much expertise do they have?
What kind of language do they use?

And then for more of those technical topics that we might not have the in-house expertise for, when we do our research, it's all stored in this knowledge base that is in a knowledge graph so that we can retrieve it semantically and like speed up the process with our content creation.
And so like history of Django, we're using like specialized research tools, Excel, Tably.
We're using multiple different models as we're creating content.
And then when you look at like a piece of content.
And

it's actually being created, you can see that, you know, for this piece on refactoring, like a technical guide, we're doing 38 minutes of agentic research, and when you look at the actual workflow, you'll see that we break this down into these different steps, right, where, similar to generating the brief, we're actually using all of these different inputs that we have, in addition to those different tools.
And then we're first generating like a research plan on, who is this research for?
What's the objective?
What is the success criteria?
What are those source requirements that we built into the artifacts?

And then as we go in and actually answer all these research questions, synthesize results, we'll run this agentically so that if there's gaps, we actually run the research again, know, 38 minutes of research, to hit the research that we have, to store the research in the platforms that we
We can use it the next time we create a piece of content, and then we are, like, really learning from every draft that we create with the client.

So, you know, it's hard to, like, answer the question in, like, one, you know, sort of, like, quick two-minute synopsis, but the idea is just that we can customize the workflows and we can build a lot of context into our artifacts so that when we're publishing content, like, it can meet the bar of the client.

The key gem is just that if the client expects us from day one to write like their developers would write, it's probably a bad fit, you know, because that's not, like, necessarily what's optimized for SEO and GEO.
That's what's optimized for thought leadership for the company, right, which we can free up their internal resources to execute on by creating a lot of this content at scale.
Yeah, but the issue, like, I hear you, but the kind of the tension is always, like, if it's slop, it may be good for GEO, but that's...

That impacts your brand, like that, that kind of, like we do password security, if there's like a mistake, and it's like sloppy, like you, it may work, like the AI search engines might like it, but your brand has just gotten dinged badly, yeah.
For sure, for sure.
And I mean, that's why we have experts in the loop.
That's why we have like a big delivery team.
And that's why like with every piece of content we write, we run this fact checker so that every single passage, we're doing more deep research.
And if we can't validate that it's accurate, we'll actually like suggest a rewrite that the editor can then accept.
And so, you know, as we're actually creating the content, our team is making a lot of changes directly to it, or using all the context to speed up the process, right?
And instead of like one-shotting it through ChatGPT, they're breaking it down into distinct steps and, you know, add three bullet points that are- Okay, this is good.
Actually, I got to interrupt in a second.
I got to go about- Actually-

So interesting, I definitely get what's going on, and I'm going to characterize this as a comprehensive, amazing content partner, is what I would say you are, and you really have some amazing premium clients.

So it definitely makes sense to understand you are helpful, and there may very well be companies in our portfolio who are like, oh my gosh, this is exactly what I'm looking for, so really glad we've chatted.
You know, let me kind of like circle back with the companies out there, and if there's some that are fit, you know, connecting, we can go from there.
Yeah, that sounds great, Jim.

I would say, you know, if there's a need on editorial, if there's a belief that, you know, they need to scale SEO, GEO content, if there is like a desire to leverage AI with content creation, we're a great fit.
Yeah, awesome.
Regardless of technical sophistication, yeah.
Right, okay, cool.
All right, thank you for the time.
I really appreciate it.
I hope you have a very nice Thanksgiving.
Likewise.
Good to meet you.
Thank you.
Bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Evaluate GrowthX as a content partner for Francisco Partners' portfolio companies.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=52.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**The Need:** Francisco Partners (FPO) needs a new AI search content partner after its top choice, X Funnel, was acquired by HubSpot.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=52.0)
  - [**The Solution:** GrowthX is an end-to-end content agency using AI workflows and human experts to scale high-quality, on-brand content for SEO and LLM visibility.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=171.0)
  - [**The Model:** A two-phase engagement: an 8-week strategy sprint to build context, followed by a 12-month execution phase targeting \~5 articles/week.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=275.0)
  - [**The Challenge:** FPO's B2B SaaS companies require deep technical expertise. GrowthX addresses this with a proprietary workflow that combines agentic AI research, a knowledge graph, and human fact-checking to ensure accuracy and authority.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=1026.0)

## Topics

### The Problem: FPO's Content Partner Gap

  - [FPO's Marketing Operating Partner, Jim Kingsbury, needs to replace X Funnel, a top-performing AI search partner that was acquired by HubSpot.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=52.0)
  - [The portfolio has diverse needs, requiring a new set of vetted partners.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=145.0)

### The Solution: GrowthX's End-to-End Model

  - [GrowthX is an end-to-end agency that handles the full content lifecycle:](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=171.0)
      - [SEO/GEO strategy](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=171.0)
      - [Content creation](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=171.0)
      - [CMS staging & publishing](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=171.0)
      - [LLM visibility & monitoring](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=171.0)
  - [**Goal:** Enable clients to scale high-quality content that feels human-written, solving for time-to-value, quality control, and cost management.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=210.0)
  - [**Team:** AI workflows are guided by human experts, including a Chief Content Officer who was a former editor-in-chief at TechCrunch.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=226.0)

### The Process: Two-Phase Engagement

  - [**Phase 1: Strategy Sprint (8 weeks)**](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=275.0)
      - [**Goal:** Build the context engineering needed for effective AI workflows.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=335.0)
      - [**Client Commitment:** Low (1-hr kickoff, 45-min deep dive, 15-30 min CMS access).](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=275.0)
      - [**Deliverables:**](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=335.0)
          - [Context artifacts (e.g., writing guidelines, persona docs)](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=562.0)
          - [10–20 published articles](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=335.0)
          - [3–6 month content roadmap](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=335.0)
  - [**Phase 2: Growth Execution (12 months)**](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=304.0)
      - [**Goal:** Scale content production based on the roadmap.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=353.0)
      - [**Production:** Targets \~5 articles/week.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=356.0)
      - [**Workflow:** GrowthX handles all execution; clients only review drafts and provide feedback.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=484.0)
      - [**Scope Expansion:** Typically starts with editorial (blog) and expands to programmatic content (e.g., vendor directories, calculators) or distribution channels (e.g., newsletters).](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=360.0)

### The Challenge: Ensuring Technical Authority

  - [**FPO's Concern:** B2B SaaS companies require deep technical expertise to avoid publishing content that damages their brand, even if it performs well for SEO.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=1026.0)
  - [**GrowthX's Solution:** A multi-step workflow designed for technical accuracy.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=1026.0)
    1.  [**Artifacts:** Initial strategy sprint defines brand guidelines, personas, and source requirements.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=562.0)
    2.  [**Agentic Research:** AI agents conduct deep research (e.g., 38 mins for one guide), using tools like Excel and Tably, and re-running searches to fill gaps.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=1198.0)
    3.  [**Knowledge Graph:** Research is stored for semantic retrieval, speeding up future content creation.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=1171.0)
    4.  [**Fact-Checking:** An automated fact-checker validates every passage; human editors review and approve suggested rewrites for inaccuracies.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=1310.0)
    5.  [**Focus:** The goal is authoritative, SEO-optimized content. GrowthX can free up internal experts to focus on thought leadership.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=1288.0)

### The Proof: Client Success & ROI

  - [**Key Metrics:** GrowthX tracks ROI via traffic, engagement, and conversions, reported in custom Looker dashboards.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=928.0)
  - [**Example Results:**](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=928.0)
      - [**Augment Code:** Tripled organic traffic since starting in June.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=944.0)
      - [**Ramp:** Built a programmatic vendor directory and per diem calculator, expanding Ramp's search presence to competitive terms (e.g., "ClickUp vs. Airtable").](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=720.0)
      - [**Webflow:** Started with developer docs and is now expanding to a templates library.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=869.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Jim Kingsbury (FPO):** Evaluate GrowthX against portfolio company needs and connect any potential fits.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=1382.0)
  - [**Tyler Pavlas (GrowthX):** Await potential introductions from FPO.](https://fathom.video/share/v3GzEafyMog8LJZS69xFSXLT_GLf64em?tab=summary&timestamp=1382.0)

action_items.description: Email FP portfolio companies re: GrowthX; if fit, intro Tyler
